items.passage: Some animals live in the deep sea where almost no sunlight ever reaches. They use other senses to find food and friends in the cold dark water. Many of them make their own light to attract small fish that they can eat. Their soft bodies help them survive the heavy pressure pushing down from above. Most of them never come up to the warm shallow water near the surface.

Desertification happens when good farmland slowly turns into dry land where almost nothing can grow. It often begins when people cut too many trees or let animals eat all the grass. Without roots in the soil, the wind blows away the dust that plants need. Heavy rain can also wash the rich top layer down the hills into rivers. Local farmers then have to move to new places to find food.

When people feel stressed, their bodies quickly get ready to act fast. The heart starts to beat much faster and breathing becomes more rapid than before. These changes help blood reach the muscles so a person can run or fight. The brain also stays very alert during this short period of time. After the danger passes, most bodies slowly return to a normal calm state.

Ancient farmers watched the sky to know when to plant their crops each year. They noticed that some bright stars came back at the same time every season. Many groups built large stone circles that lined up with the rising sun. These early sky watchers also drew maps of the moon as it changed shape. Their careful notes helped later thinkers learn about the planets in our sky.

Minerals are solid materials that form deep inside the rocky parts of the earth. They have a special shape because their tiny parts join together in a regular pattern. Some of them grow very slowly inside hot pools of liquid rock far below us. Others appear when warm water leaves salt and other bits behind in old caves. People dig them up to use in tools, jewelry, and many household items.

Asteroid belts are wide rings of small rocky bodies that move around our sun. The largest belt sits between the orbits of Mars and the giant planet Jupiter. Most of these rocks are tiny but a few are large enough to have their own name. They have stayed there for a long time without joining to form a planet. Scientists send small space probes to study what these rocks are made of.
